transcript.text: Hello, what is up? I am testing out this tool and I have to speak something for about 30 seconds so that my API is hit first. Let's go. Let me talk about something. Let me just mention what this app contains. It's called TwinMind Live Suggestions. It's a three-panel web application.

On the left, I have mic and transcript. In the middle, I have live suggestions. And on the right, I have a chat. So the way it works is I can click on the mic to record myself or anyone can just speak through while having this application on. And the transcript is being taken by the mic and it

It's being sent to the Grok API. And it does some SST, speech to text transcription, and returns back the text. And takes about 30 seconds because I call the API every 30 seconds. So everything I speak is being stored into a blog and is being sent to the API every 30 seconds. Next, life suggestions are suggestions

given by the AI. So it goes through the transcript and it reads out what could be possible questions to ask, what's a talking point, and if there's something to be clarified, it gives out a couple of suggestions to keep the conversation going or to keep it more interesting. And this is also being powered by Grok. So all the transcripts

that's being transcribed by the voice API is being stored in the context and we are sending that to live suggestions. So there's every 30 seconds, we make the AI generate a couple of suggestions. So right now I'm just seeing that it has three suggestions for me. It has a question to ask suggestion. that's

is how our transcript transcription errors handled okay so interesting how are transcriptions handled it has tells me ask about error correction mechanisms or confidence scores and it gives me a talking point real-time transcription for meeting minutes highlight that live text can be edited

on the fly and instantly share with participants. Interesting. Where is audio and transcript data stored? Yeah, so that's a good question. It is being stored within the local storage as of now. Since this is a demo, I don't really need a database. And local storage has enough storage. I think it's about five.
MB of storage where I can store a lot of text and do some context management. Data privacy. So I don't really do a lot of encryption. All I do is...

pretty much converted into a web blob so that the browser is doing most of the work and we just send the blob to the grok api. So we are pretty much sending it sending all my audio to grok but should be fine because it's it is text but it's a great question to be asked on to
where it requires a lot of privacy. So that's a good question.
Mm hmm. Mm hmm. Mm hmm.

suggestions.title: What does each panel display?
suggestions.preview: Ask the group to outline the specific content shown in the left, middle, and right panels of TwinMind Live Suggestions.

suggestions.title: Highlight real‑time collaboration benefits
suggestions.preview: Mention that the middle panel streams live AI suggestions while participants can instantly act on them, boosting meeting productivity.

suggestions.title: Explain “API is hit first”
suggestions.preview: Clarify that the initial 30‑second speech triggers the backend API call that powers the live‑suggestion engine.

suggestions.title: Can we see a demo of the 30‑second API cycle?
suggestions.preview: Ask the presenter to record a short segment and show how the transcript updates after each 30‑second Grok API call.

suggestions.title: Potential latency impact on user experience
suggestions.preview: Highlight that a fixed 30‑second round‑trip may cause lag for speakers and suggest exploring streaming transcription or shorter intervals.

suggestions.title: What does ‘SST’ stand for?
suggestions.preview: Explain that SST refers to Speech‑to‑Text, the technology that converts spoken audio into written text.

suggestions.title: What are the primary use cases for TwinMind Live?
suggestions.preview: Ask the team to outline scenarios like remote meetings, webinars, or live captioning where the three‑panel layout adds value.

suggestions.title: Latency and real‑time performance metrics
suggestions.preview: Mention the target end‑to‑end latency (e.g., <500 ms) and how the system measures and optimizes it during live sessions.

suggestions.title: How transcription handles multiple speakers
suggestions.preview: Explain that the mic panel can capture separate audio streams or use speaker diarization to label each participant in the transcript.

suggestions.title: How are transcription errors handled?
suggestions.preview: Ask about error correction mechanisms or confidence scores to ensure the blog captures accurate text.

suggestions.title: Real‑time transcription for meeting minutes
suggestions.preview: Highlight that live text can be edited on the fly and instantly shared with participants, improving documentation speed.

suggestions.title: Where is audio and transcript data stored?
suggestions.preview: Specify whether recordings and transcriptions are saved locally, in a cloud bucket, or only temporarily before posting to the blog.

suggestions.title: What integration options does TwinMind support?
suggestions.preview: Ask about available APIs, SDKs, or webhook mechanisms to embed TwinMind Live Suggestions into existing platforms.

suggestions.title: Benefit of auto‑archiving transcripts to a blog
suggestions.preview: Emphasize that storing spoken content as searchable blog posts creates a persistent knowledge base and improves team documentation.

suggestions.title: How does the system differentiate multiple speakers?
suggestions.preview: Provide details on whether speaker diarization is performed, how speakers are labeled, and any limitations in mixed‑speaker scenarios.

suggestions.title: API call interval vs typical latency
suggestions.preview: The app calls the Grok API every 30 seconds, but most speech‑to‑text services return results in under 5 seconds; a shorter interval could improve responsiveness.

suggestions.title: How is user data stored and protected?
suggestions.preview: Ask the team what encryption, retention policy, and access controls are applied to the audio and transcript data.

suggestions.title: Add speaker diarization for clearer multi‑speaker logs
suggestions.preview: Suggest implementing speaker identification so the transcript tags each speaker, improving readability and downstream analysis.

suggestions.title: Transcription error handling and confidence scores
suggestions.preview: The Grok SST API returns a confidence score for each transcribed segment; low‑confidence portions can be flagged for manual correction or automatically refined using a language‑model post‑processor.

suggestions.title: Should we enable real‑time speaker diarization?
suggestions.preview: Ask the group if adding speaker diarization is a priority to improve attribution of remarks in the live suggestions and meeting minutes.

suggestions.title: Latency impact of 30‑second batching on meeting flow
suggestions.preview: Highlight that batching API calls every 30 seconds reduces load but introduces a delay in suggestions; discuss whether a shorter or adaptive interval would benefit real‑time interaction.

suggestions.title: Transcription error handling & confidence scores
suggestions.preview: The Grok API returns a confidence score for each word. Segments below a set threshold are flagged, allowing users to edit or request re‑transcription, ensuring accurate final text.

suggestions.title: What confidence threshold should we set for auto‑accepting text?
suggestions.preview: Propose a confidence score (e.g., 0.85) above which transcript lines are auto‑accepted, and below which they are marked for manual review.

suggestions.title: Live editing syncs back to stored blog for clean minutes
suggestions.preview: Emphasize that any edits made to the live transcript are immediately reflected in the blog archive, giving participants a reliable source for final meeting minutes.

suggestions.title: Browser localStorage size limit
suggestions.preview: Typical browsers cap localStorage at ~5 MB per origin, not 5 GB. Verify your app’s storage assumptions.

suggestions.title: Plan for persistent storage beyond demo
suggestions.preview: Ask the team how they intend to migrate transcripts from localStorage to a database or cloud bucket for production use.

suggestions.title: Data privacy & compliance for stored transcripts
suggestions.preview: Highlight the need to encrypt stored audio/transcripts and define retention policies to meet GDPR or CCPA requirements.

suggestions.title: Audio & transcript storage location
suggestions.preview: The demo stores both audio blobs and transcribed text in the browser's localStorage as a Web Blob, roughly 5 MB per origin, with no server‑side persistence.

suggestions.title: LocalStorage size limit accuracy
suggestions.preview: Most modern browsers cap localStorage at about 5 MB per origin (some up to 10 MB), so the 5 MB figure mentioned is generally correct.

suggestions.title: Should we encrypt stored transcripts?
suggestions.preview: Is client‑side encryption of the localStorage blob feasible to protect transcript privacy before sending audio to the Grok API?

chat.content:
[answer] Audio & transcript storage location

Preview: The demo stores both audio blobs and transcribed text in the browser's localStorage as a Web Blob, roughly 5 MB per origin, with no server‑side persistence.

The demo stores both audio blobs and transcribed text locally in the browser’s localStorage as a Web Blob.  
- LocalStorage capacity is ~5 MB per origin, enough for many minutes of text.  
- No server‑side database is used; everything stays client‑side.  
- Audio is sent to the Grok API as a blob, then the returned transcript is saved locally.  
- No encryption or additional privacy measures are applied in this demo.  
(Triggered by “Where is audio and transcript data stored?”)
